text:
Abstract

Mineral surfaces can govern the activity of extracellular enzymes in soils and sediments, and have generally been reported to provide a protective role, which leads to a prolonged activity (i.e., longer activity lifetime). This study used the model enzyme β-glucosidase (BG) and reduced iron-bearing minerals (nontronite, montmorillonite and magnetite) to investigate how the widespread process of oxygenation of reduced iron-bearing minerals which leads to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) affects enzyme adsorption and activity. Our results showed that although enzyme adsorption under anoxic conditions enhanced enzyme stability, addition of oxygen under the anoxic adsorbed BG resulted in the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and other ROS. The amount of •OH produced was closely related to the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation in the minerals. The ROS induced structural change and degradation of the BG enzyme, resulting in severe enzyme inactivation and decreased enzyme functional lifetime shortening. Our results showed that in iron-rich and oxygen-rich environments, the effect of ROS on enzyme inactivation can counteract the enzyme adsorption and surface protection effect, thus directly affecting the stability of active enzyme pool and thus impacting carbon cycle in redox-oscillating environments.
